posts.text:
DNN's are "automatic feature learners", so even fundamentally statistical learning algorithms like variational methods are considered ML because they invoke the neural toolbox.

The term ML basically subsumes all of statistics today.

reply_context.text: These tools are fundamentally resampling methods and do still quantify uncertainty. They are statistical learning tools developed first by computer scientists. The "machine" in "ML" now just means any automation of the construction of heuristics. ...

posts.text: These tools are fundamentally resampling methods and do still quantify uncertainty. They are statistical learning tools developed first by computer scientists. The "machine" in "ML" now just means any automation of the construction of heuristics. ...

reply_context.text: About 20 yrs ago there was a revolution in predictive analytics resulting from the discovery that ensembles of weak trees (aka forests) were extremely effective. This led to methods that had traditionally been considered "ML" dominating the predictive analytics toolbox. ...

posts.text: About 20 yrs ago there was a revolution in predictive analytics resulting from the discovery that ensembles of weak trees (aka forests) were extremely effective. This led to methods that had traditionally been considered "ML" dominating the predictive analytics toolbox. ...

reply_context.text:
I'd argue that statistics is fundamentally the art of quantifying uncertainty. This subsumes predictive analytics where you are hoping to make statements subject to risk.

"Machine" decision making implies (or at least used to) deterministic heuristics, i.e. decision trees. ...

reply_context.text: I’ve always thought of the distinction between statistics and machine learning as: statistics is about inferring facts whereas machine learning is about predicting things. But as I think about it, statistics is one of the few domains where uncertainty is at least sometimes acknowledged publicly
